title: Research for Public Policy - Dr. Colin Kuehl
text:
Welcome to the course website for Research Methodology for Public Policy.
This course introduces the fundamental concepts of research design and data analysis using R. No prior programming experience required.

title: Research Methodology for Public Policy
text:
Instructor: Dr. Colin Kuehl (Northern Illinois University)
Email: ckuehl@niu.edu
Office Hours: TBD and by appointment | Office: TBD
Course Meetings: Monday-Thursday 10:30-12 & 1-2:230

title: Research Methodology for Public Policy
text:
Prerequisites
I assume only high school algebra and a tolerance for stubborn work. Regarding the latter, getting your head around the material and making your statistical software package do what you want it to do can sometimes be both frustrating and time consuming. I can only encourage you to keep trying. With persistence (and sometimes a little help), you will eventually figure it out. This is an essential part of the process. Regarding the former, calculus is helpful since some of the material we cover makes use of it. However, don’t panic: it is not necessary. I will show you some math from time to time in lecture, but this will be solely to provide you with motivating, behind-the-scenes intuition. You are not expected to be able to.

title: Research Methodology for Public Policy
text:
My Classroom
This class is derived from courses I’ve taught at American universities for many years. As such, it will be taught using the American style of graduate education. That means an emphasis on high expectations, critical thinking, collective endeavor, and a blurring of academic rank. We are colleagues and so please help in creating an intellectually engaging, open, and, hopefully, fun, environment in which we all learn a great deal.
It is also important to note that quality social science research is more akin to art than a series of checklists. Accordingly, I will be emphasizing the intuition and logic of research over the minutia of statistical tests or specific processes. There are many ways to conduct quality research, often depending on the question,. This class is intended to provide an introduction to a wide variety of these approaches rather than being a guide to mastering a particular one.

title: Research Methodology for Public Policy
text:
A Note on Statistical Software
Knowledge of statistical software (or qualitative software) is an increasingly important component of any political scientist’s toolbox. The choice of statistical software is one of continuous debate. However, most quantitative researchers today use either STATA, Python, or R to complete their analysis.
This course will be taught using R for a number of reasons. 1. Its free! This allows students to use R on their home computer, laptop, etc and ensures access to R long past your time here at CMU. 2. Cutting edge political methods work is done using R. 3. The things you can do using R (and associated packages) is far broader than in STATA/SPSS/etc or and it is far more approachable than Python. 4. Basic computer coding and understanding coding structures is an increasingly important skill across a number of domains that, I hope, will you will continue to use throughout your career.
For those interested, the course will also provide an introduction to the use of Markdown document preparation software.

title: Research Methodology for Public Policy
text:
Course Meetings
Each day will be divided into two sessions. The morning portion(1030-12) will be a combination of lecture and discussion on fundamental concepts in public policy research. The afternoon session will focus on application of research techniques with a particular focus on coding in R. Any remaining time in the afternoon session will be used to work on problem sets and your final project.

title: Research Methodology for Public Policy
text:
Readings
Readings for the course will be a combination of open access textbook chapters and online articles. All students are expected to complete each reading prior to the start of class. See the schedule below for the complete list.
We will have three textbooks for the course. All three are open-access and available totally free at the following links. All books will be referred to

EMPS - Clipperton, et al (2022) Empirical methods in political science: An introduction Northwestern University
QRM - Hurst 2023 Introduction to Qualitative Research Methods Oregon State University
QUANT - Jenkins Smith, et al. Quantitative Research Methods for Political Science, Public Policy and Public Administration: 4th Edition With Applications in R University of Oklahoma

All other course readings will be available on the course website or through email.

title: Research Methodology for Public Policy
text:
Grading

Final Project (40%): Your final project will be an independent quantitative analysis of a public policy topic of your choosing. You will formulate a hypothesis, conduct basic exploratory data analysis and perform, as well as correctly interpret, a multivariate regression. You will present your results to the class at the end of the semester and write up what you found in the form of short report. A research proposal and preliminary analysis in earlier weeks will help you build toward your final paper. An assignment sheet will be provided and we will discuss details in the following weeks.
Problem Sets (20%): Throughout the semester you will be expected to complete a number of problem sets. These exercises will give you a chance to apply the ideas and concepts using the R coding we are learning. Students are allowed, even encouraged, to help each other on problem sets. However, the work itself must done each individual student. Students will not be allowed to work together on the midterm or final paper.

Problem Set Due Dates

Problem Set #1: July 8th
Problem Set #2: July 10th
Problem Set #3: July 15th
Problem Set #4: July 17th
Problem Set #5: Nov. 22nd


Midterm (20%): July 16th - Morning Session Students will be expected to demonstrate understanding about both concepts in public policy research and coding independently in R. More details to follow.
Participation (20%): This is not a lecture course. Participation includes active class discussion and engagement during the practicum, including asking questions when you have them. Participation goes well beyond attendance. It includes thoughtful engagement with all parts of the class. This course is taught around class discussion, debate, and activities. This means we will all be dependent on each other’s efforts to prepare for and engage in class activities and have a worthwhile learning experience.Speaking the language of methodology is an essential component of your graduate training.

title: Research Methodology for Public Policy
text:
Course Structure
The tentative schedule for the course is below. Some topics will take just a week, others more. Given our small size and wide variety of backgrounds, we will be flexible in how fast we move. If we move quickly, there are a number of optional topics we can cover at the end of the course. Treat this as a preliminary overview, and not a complete roadmap. Changes to reading eading assignments (beyond your book chapters) for the following week will be provided at the end of each class

title: Research Methodology for Public Policy
text:
Additional Recommended Readings
Many students want to read more on these topics. For some, it is because they are nervous to take a “math” class and want to do some reading to feel prepared. Some take the class and find that they love it so much they want to learn more. For those students (sometimes its the same student) the books below are excellent resources. If you have more questions please reach out to me.

“Pop” data science books:

Carl Bergstrom & Jevin West – Calling Bullshit
Nate Silver – The Signal and the Noise
Charles Wheelan – Naked Statistics
Tim Harford – The Data Detective



Basic Introductions

Neil Salkind – Statistics for People who (think they) Hate Statistics
Scott Cunningham – Causal Inference: The Mixtape
Gary Klass – Just Plain Data Analysis



Advanced Texts
For students looking to go beyond what is taught in the course:

Andrew Gelman and Jennifer Hill – Data Analysis Using Regression and Multilevel/Hierarchical Models
Damodar N. Gujarati – Essentials of Econometrics
Jeffrey Wooldridge – Introductory Econometrics
Elena Llaudet and Kosuke Imai – Data Analysis for Social Science
Jenine Harris – Statistics with R
Guido Imbens and Donald Rubin – Causal Inference for Statistics, Social, and Biomedical Sciences

title: R Code
text: Code used in class will be posted here. Download to your own computer for use in class

title: R Code
text:
Setup: Installing R & RStudio

Install R from cran.r-project.org
Install RStudio from posit.co

title: R Code
text:
Week 1–2: Getting Started with R
Download script
# ── Basics ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
2 + 2          # arithmetic
sqrt(144)      # functions
x <- 42        # assignment
x * 2

# ── Vectors ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
scores <- c(88, 92, 75, 98, 61, 84)
length(scores)
mean(scores)
sd(scores)
summary(scores)

# ── Loading data ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
library(tidyverse)
data <- read_csv("data/survey.csv")
glimpse(data)
head(data)

title: R Code
text:
Week 3–4: Descriptive Statistics & Visualization
Download script
library(tidyverse)
data <- read_csv("data/survey.csv")

# ── Histogram ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ggplot(data, aes(x = age)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 5, fill = "steelblue", color = "white") +
  labs(title = "Age Distribution", x = "Age", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal()

# ── Boxplot ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ggplot(data, aes(x = major, y = gpa, fill = major)) +
  geom_boxplot(show.legend = FALSE) +
  labs(title = "GPA by Major", x = "Major", y = "GPA") +
  theme_minimal()

# ── Bar chart ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ggplot(data, aes(x = year)) +
  geom_bar(fill = "coral") +
  labs(title = "Students by Year", x = "Year", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal()

# ── Numerical summaries by group ─────────────────────────────────────────
data |>
  group_by(major) |>
  summarise(
    n    = n(),
    mean = mean(gpa, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd   = sd(gpa,   na.rm = TRUE),
    med  = median(gpa, na.rm = TRUE)
  )

title: R Code
text:
Week 5–6: Probability & Distributions
Download script
# ── Normal distribution ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
# P(X < 70) where X ~ N(75, 10)
pnorm(70, mean = 75, sd = 10)

# Find the 90th percentile
qnorm(0.90, mean = 75, sd = 10)

# ── Visualize normal curve ────────────────────────────────────────────────
x_vals <- seq(40, 110, length.out = 300)
y_vals <- dnorm(x_vals, mean = 75, sd = 10)
curve_df <- data.frame(x = x_vals, y = y_vals)

ggplot(curve_df, aes(x, y)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1, color = "steelblue") +
  geom_area(data = subset(curve_df, x < 70),
            fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.3) +
  labs(title = "P(X < 70) shaded", x = "Score", y = "Density") +
  theme_minimal()

# ── Binomial ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# P(X = 3) where X ~ Binom(10, 0.4)
dbinom(3, size = 10, prob = 0.4)

# P(X <= 5)
pbinom(5, size = 10, prob = 0.4)

title: R Code
text:
Week 8–10: Confidence Intervals & Hypothesis Testing
Download script
library(tidyverse)

# ── One-sample t-test & confidence interval ───────────────────────────────
scores <- c(72, 85, 91, 68, 79, 88, 74, 95, 83, 77)

t.test(scores, mu = 75, conf.level = 0.95)

# ── Two-sample t-test ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
group_a <- c(82, 88, 74, 91, 79, 85)
group_b <- c(70, 75, 68, 80, 72, 77)

t.test(group_a, group_b, var.equal = FALSE)

# ── Proportion test ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# 54 successes out of 100 trials; test H0: p = 0.5
prop.test(x = 54, n = 100, p = 0.5)

# ── Chi-square test ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
observed <- matrix(c(30, 20, 25, 35, 15, 25), nrow = 2)
chisq.test(observed)

title: R Code
text:
Week 13–14: Linear Regression
Download script
library(tidyverse)
data <- read_csv("data/survey.csv")

# ── Scatterplot ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ggplot(data, aes(x = study_hours, y = exam_score)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6, color = "steelblue") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, color = "coral") +
  labs(title = "Exam Score vs. Study Hours",
       x = "Hours Studied per Week", y = "Exam Score") +
  theme_minimal()

# ── Fit model ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
model <- lm(exam_score ~ study_hours, data = data)
summary(model)

# ── Diagnostic plots ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
plot(model)
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

# ── Prediction ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
new_data <- data.frame(study_hours = c(5, 10, 15))
predict(model, newdata = new_data, interval = "confidence")







TipUsing These Scripts



Save each .R file in your project folder alongside your data/ directory. Open in RStudio and run line-by-line with Ctrl+Enter (Windows/Linux) or Cmd+Enter (Mac).

title: Final Project
text:
Project Requirements

Dataset

At least 100 observations and 5 variables
Must be real data (not simulated)
Suggested sources: TidyTuesday, Kaggle, UCI ML Repository, government open data portals
You must have a research question your analysis addresses



Report (rendered as HTML from .qmd)
Your Quarto report must include all of the following sections:

Introduction — Background, research question, and why it matters.
Data — Source, collection method, variables of interest, and any cleaning steps.
Exploratory Analysis — At least 3 visualizations with written interpretation.
Inferential Analysis — At least one formal statistical test or model (confidence interval, hypothesis test, or regression).
Conclusion — Summary of findings, limitations, and potential next steps.
References — Dataset citation and any other sources.

All code must appear in the rendered document (echo: true).


Presentation (10 minutes)

Slides built in Quarto (format: revealjs recommended)
Cover your question, key findings, and one visualization
7 minutes presenting + 3 minutes Q&A

title: Final Project
text:
Grading Rubric



Component
Points




Proposal (clear question + viable dataset)
10


Exploratory analysis (3+ visualizations, interpretation)
20


Inferential analysis (correct method, interpretation)
25


Writing quality (clarity, structure, flow)
15


Reproducibility (renders without errors, code shown)
15


Presentation (clarity, visuals, Q&A)
15


Total
100

title: Final Project
text:
Report Template
Start from this template to ensure your report has the required structure.
---
title: "Your Project Title"
author: "Your Name"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-fold: false
    echo: true
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
---

## Introduction

[Describe your research question and why it is interesting.]

## Data

```{r}
library(tidyverse)
data <- read_csv("your_data.csv")
glimpse(data)
```

[Describe the dataset: source, variables, observations.]

## Exploratory Analysis

```{r}
# Visualization 1
```

[Interpret the plot.]

## Inferential Analysis

```{r}
# Your statistical test or model
```

[Interpret results in context.]

## Conclusion

[Summarize findings, discuss limitations, suggest future work.]

## References

- Dataset: [Source name and URL]

title: Final Project
text:
Proposal Guidelines
Your one-page proposal should answer:

What is your research question? Be specific (e.g., “Is there a relationship between sleep hours and GPA among college students?” rather than “I want to study sleep”).
What dataset will you use? Provide the source URL and describe the key variables.
What statistical methods do you plan to use? Reference specific methods from class.

Submit as a PDF on Canvas by March 28 at 11:59pm.







ImportantAcademic Integrity



All analysis must be your own work. Properly cite your data source. If you use code from external sources (Stack Overflow, documentation, etc.), include a comment with the URL. Passing off others’ analysis or AI-generated code as your own is a violation of the academic integrity policy.

title: Final Project
text:
Past Project Examples
Below are a few examples of strong projects from previous semesters (shared with permission):

Predicting Avocado Prices with Linear Regression — used USDA retail price data
Racial Disparities in Traffic Stops: A Hypothesis Testing Approach — used Stanford Open Policing Project data
Does Weather Affect Bike-Share Ridership? — used Chicago Divvy trip data with NOAA weather data

title: Problem Sets

title: Problem Sets
text:
Submission Guidelines

Render your completed .qmd file to HTML and submit the .html output on Canvas.
Include your name and problem set number in the YAML header of your document.
Show all code (echo: true). Answers without supporting code will not receive credit.
Late submissions are accepted up to 48 hours after the deadline with a 20% deduction. Work submitted after 48 hours will not be graded.







TipStarter File YAML Header



Add this to the top of your .qmd file before submitting:
---
title: "Problem Set 1"
author: "Your Name"
date: today
format:
  html:
    echo: true
    toc: true
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
---
